Director/PDMR Shareholding
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Limited (AIM: MPL), which is operating and continuing to develop a port and logistics facility in Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, announces that that on 9 March 2026, Jeremy Warner Allen, Non-Executive Chairman of the Company, has transferred, via sale and purchase (the "Transfer"), Ordinary Shares into his ISA. Mr Allen sold 3,827,494 Ordinary Shares at a price of 0.313 pence and purchased 3,827,494 Ordinary Shares at a price of 0.314 pence to be held in his ISA.
Following the transfer, Mr Allen's beneficial shareholding remains unchanged at 3,827,994 Ordinary Shares, representing
1.07
% of the Company's issued share capital.

latest.title: Update on Ongoing Legal Proceedings
latest.content:
18 Dec 2025 15:29
RNS Number : 1776M
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Ltd
18 December 2025
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Limited
(
"MPL"
or the
"Company"
)
Update
Mercantile
Ports & Logistics Limited (AIM: MPL), which is operating and continuing to develop a port and logistics facility in Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, provides
an update on the ongoing legal proceedings relating to its subsidiary, Karanja Terminal & Logistics Pvt. Ltd.
A civil appeal was listed before the Supreme Court of India earlier today, in which the appellant sought to set aside the interim order previously granted in MPL's favour by the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) on 10 December 2025.
Following submissions from all parties, the Supreme Court did not pass any adverse order against the Company and directed the NCLAT to hear the matter on 14 January 2026, and, if possible, to dispose of the appeal on that date.
Importantly, the interim order dated 10 December 2025 remains in force.
The Company will continue to pursue all legal remedies available to protect its interests and will provide further updates as appropriate.
For further information, please visit
www.mercpl.com
or contact:
MPL
c/o Cavendish
+44 (0) 207 220 0500
Cavendish Capital Markets Limited
(Nomad and Broker)
Stephen Keys
+44 (0) 207 220 0500

latest.title: Update
latest.content:
17 Dec 2025 09:37
RNS Number : 9350L
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Ltd
17 December 2025
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Limited
(
"MPL"
or the
"Company"
)
Update
Mercantile
Ports & Logistics Limited (AIM: MPL), which is operating and continuing to develop a port and logistics facility in Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, provides an update on the legal position and
confirms that it continues to pursue vigorously all legal remedies to safeguard its wholly owned strategic asset, Karanja Terminal & Logistics Pvt. Ltd. ("KTPL"), located in Navi Mumbai, India.
The Company recently secured interim protection from the Insolvency Appellate Tribunal, which restrains the solicitation of expressions of interest from third parties, thereby preserving the status quo while legal proceedings are ongoing.
MPL has also formally offered to redeem the full outstanding debt relating to the asset, exercising its lawful right to settlement. The debt had previously been assigned to Prudent ARC, a transaction which MPL is contesting and which forms part of ongoing legal proceedings.
Prudent ARC has since appealed against the interim order to the Supreme Court of India. The matter is scheduled to be heard on Thursday, 18 December 2025.
MPL remains confident in the strength of its legal position and is committed to defending shareholder value while ensuring that due process is followed.
The Company continues to enjoy the support of its major global stakeholders.
Commenting on the development, Pav Bakhshi, Managing Director of Mercantile Ports & Logistics, said:
"We have made a credible and lawful offer to redeem the outstanding debt in full. Karanja is a strategic infrastructure asset that rightfully belongs to MPL, and we will continue to pursue all legal avenues to protect the Company's interests and those of its shareholders."
Further announcements will be made as developments unfold.
For further information, please visit
www.mercpl.com
or contact:
MPL
c/o Cavendish
+44 (0) 207 220 0500
Cavendish Capital Markets Limited
(Nomad and Broker)
Stephen Keys
+44 (0) 207 220 0500

latest.title: Result of General Meeting
latest.content:
15 Dec 2025 11:17
RNS Number : 6006L
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Ltd
15 December 2025
Mercantile Ports & Logistics Limited
(
"MPL"
or the
"Company"
)
Result of General Meeting
Mercantile
Ports & Logistics Limited (AIM: MPL), which is operating and continuing to develop a port and logistics facility in Navi Mumbai, Maharashtra, India, is pleased to announce that further to the Company's announcement of 19 November 2025, at the General Meeting held earlier today the Resolutions proposed were duly passed by shareholders.
Application will be made to the London Stock Exchange for the cancellation of the Redemption Shares and admission of the Subscription Shares to trading on AIM. It is expected that admission of the Subscription Shares will occur and that dealings will commence at 8.00 a.m. on 16 December 2025.
Unless otherwise defined herein, capitalised terms in this announcement have the same meaning as in the shareholder circular published on 19 November 2025.
For further information, please visit
www.mercpl.com
or contact:
MPL
c/o Cavendish
+44 (0) 207 220 0500

company_30s.what_they_do: Mercantile Ports & Logistics Limited runs container terminals, freight handling and warehousing across several UK ports.  They move goods in and out of the country, offering storage, customs clearance and transport‑link services to shippers and importers.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company sits on the backbone of UK trade.  With global shipping volumes rising, efficient port operations are essential for importers, exporters and logistics firms.  Its terminals handle a share of the country’s container traffic, giving it a foothold in a market that is unlikely to shrink in the next decade.
company_30s.current_state: The business is in a survival mode – legal challenges, cash‑flow pressure and a shrinking balance sheet dominate the headlines.  It is still operating, but the company has been forced to cut costs, renegotiate contracts and look for fresh funding.  The shares trade at a fraction of their former valuation, reflecting the market’s loss of confidence.

rns_narrative: The company’s recent filings show a series of legal and financial hurdles: a court order that is still pending, an update on a loan facility that could provide a lifeline, and a director‑shareholding disclosure that hints at a possible shift in ownership control.  Together these signals paint a picture of a firm trying to patch its finances while waiting for a court decision that could either clear a major liability or add a new one.

Three themes emerge: 1) *Legal uncertainty* – the court ruling and the high‑profile interim order; 2) *Liquidity pressure* – the loan facility update and the need for fresh capital; 3) *Governance shifts* – director shareholding changes that might bring new strategic direction.
The company appears to be on the brink of a turning point: if the court clears it of a key claim, the remaining debt could be restructured, and the new ownership stake could inject fresh capital.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet waiting for a court verdict
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A few traders believe the pending ruling will remove a heavy legal burden and unlock the company’s cash flow, making a rebound plausible.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Most market observers fear that the court could side with the claimant, leaving the company with a larger debt burden and no realistic path to profitability.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The chatter has stayed flat – no sudden spikes in volume or sentiment.  The market remains silent, suggesting a lack of conviction either way.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat.  No public interest is currently pulling the stock, which means any news could create a surprise move.

near_term_catalysts: The court’s decision on the pending interim order (expected in the next 4–6 weeks).  A favourable ruling could remove a key liability and improve the balance sheet.
An announcement of a new freight contract or a lease agreement for terminal space, which would immediately raise revenue.
Any sign of fresh funding – a loan facility extension or a private equity investment – that would boost liquidity.
A sudden spike in trading volume at the 0.05p support level, indicating that a small group of investors is stepping in.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a clear legal verdict, a positive cash‑flow statement, or a sudden jump in volume above 5‑fold the daily average.  A price break above 0.06p with volume 10× the norm would be a strong confirmation of a mean‑reversion rally.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is deep in the bottom‑phase of a crash cycle – it has already shed most of its value and the market is largely indifferent.  The APEX score signals a tight compression; if the court clears the company, the next move is likely a breakout from that compressed zone.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The company sits at a 70–95% drop from its peak.  If the court ruling removes a major liability, the company could re‑establish cash flow and start turning the terminals profitable.  A bounce to 0.15p (roughly three times the current price) would double the upside – a 200% gain that outpaces the 50% risk of a 0.02p stop.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the court sides against the company, the debt load will grow, and the already thin cash reserves could evaporate.  The worst case is a further slide to 0.01p or a forced liquidation if the company cannot meet its obligations.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Risk is limited to roughly 50% of the current price if a stop is set just below the 0.05p support.  Reward potential is about 200% if the price recovers to 0.15p.  The asymmetry – a 1:4 upside‑to‑downside ratio – makes this a compelling contrarian play.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above 0.06p on volume at least 10× the average.  Size the position so that a 0.02p stop would cap losses at about 50% of the entry value.  Hold until the court ruling is announced; if it is favourable, consider adding to the position.
playbook.if_watching: Watch the court’s decision and any new funding announcement.  A positive legal outcome or a fresh loan offer will be the trigger to step in.
playbook.if_passing: None identified.  There is no imminent delisting or bankruptcy filing.  The main risk is a negative court ruling, but that would simply push the price lower – a scenario already priced into the current level.

ai_insights.bottom_line: A deep crash has left Mercantile Ports & Logistics in a silent, low‑price state; a favourable court ruling within weeks could unlock liquidity and trigger a 200% upside, making this a high‑reward, low‑downside contrarian bet.
